title: The Edgar Wright Rankings and ‘Last Night in Soho’ With Edgar Wright!
format: Rob Harvilla joins to rank Edgar Wright's filmography, then Edgar himself calls in to talk 'Last Night in Soho,' the 1960s ghosts he can't shake, and shooting mirror tricks in camera instead of on a green screen.

segments.heading: Talking Edgar Wright
segments.note: with Rob Harvilla, host of 60 Songs That Explain the '90s

films.title: Ant-Man
films.note: Harvilla swears the shrinking-suitcase fight scored to the Cure is pure Edgar Wright. It's actually Peyton Reed, who finished the film after Wright departed.

films.title: The Adventures of Tintin
films.note: Wright co-wrote it; Fennessey hasn't gotten around to it.

films.title: A Fistful of Fingers
films.note: Wright's undistributed first feature, a spaghetti-western spoof shot for cheap (findable on YouTube).

films.title: Dead Right
films.note: A student-film 'Dirty Harry' parody Wright made in college.

films.title: Don't
films.note: Wright's fake trailer inside Grindhouse, a horror homage to Hammer films of the '60s and '70s.

segments.heading: Rob Harvilla's Edgar Wright Rankings
segments.note: best to worst; Fennessey mostly agreed, give or take whichever Cornetto film he'd watched most recently
segments.subject: Edgar Wright's filmography

films.title: Scott Pilgrim vs. the World
films.note: No. 1. A box-office flop at the time that both hosts say has aged into a consensus favorite. Harvilla still watches the blooper reel on a loop.

films.title: Shaun of the Dead
films.note: The record-throwing scene sold Harvilla for life.

films.title: The World's End
films.note: Harvilla admits he's overrating it because he can't stop turning it over in his head.

films.title: Hot Fuzz
films.note: Last of the Cornetto trilogy, not for lack of love. Harvilla figures the hate mail is coming.

films.title: The Sparks Brothers
films.note: Wright's Sparks documentary, discussed at length on an earlier episode.

films.title: Baby Driver
films.note: A hit Harvilla admires more than he loves: all style, not much left over to chew on afterward.

segments.heading: Edgar Wright on 'Last Night in Soho'

films.title: Last Night in Soho
films.note: His first film centered on a woman's perspective, co-written with Krysty Wilson-Cairns; a decade-long obsession with Soho's dueling glamour and darkness, built around unbroken in-camera mirror choreography rather than green screen.

films.title: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
films.note: Wright cites an unused deleted-scene mirror shot. He later mentioned it to James Cameron directly.

films.title: Poltergeist III
films.note: Practical mirror-shot trickery Wright studied frame by frame with the film's director, Gary Sherman, over Zoom.

films.title: Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde
films.note: Rouben Mamoulian's transformation mirror trick, an inspiration Wright cites for Soho's doubling effects.

films.title: Orpheus
films.note: Jean Cocteau's mirror-as-portal imagery, another reference point.

films.title: Caught
films.note: One of the Max Ophuls noirs Wright had already seen before diving into the rest of his filmography.

films.title: The Reckless Moment
films.note: The other Ophuls noir Wright already knew.

films.title: Letter From an Unknown Woman
films.note: Part of Wright's deep dive into Ophuls last year, his pick for the last great thing he'd seen.

films.title: The Earrings of Madame de...
films.note: Fennessey pitches it as a double feature with Soho for the camerawork alone.

TV: What We Do in the Shadows
